data.title: VIS: An Opportunity To Gain From The Manufacturing Slump To The Next Rebound
data.description:
Introduction

Manufacturing activity in the U.S. has been weak for over two years after a rapid expansion witnessed in the post-pandemic rebound.

Of course, manufacturing activity shows cyclicality, and we are used to seeing cycles of expansion followed by recessions. This time, however, it strikes us particularly because both the Biden and the Trump administrations have underlined in different ways how it is strategic for the U.S. to reshore manufacturing plants. So, even though the government pours billions into combating inflation and fuel manufacturing (both through public spending and fiscal or trade policy), we are still not seeing signs of a convincing rebound. Part of this may be explained through the pull-forward effect that would make us consider the post-pandemic surge as a phenomenon that anticipated a lot of demand for goods that would have been spread over a longer timeframe.

At the same time, we are seeing a slow recovery from the 2010 lows of U.S. manufacturing employment, as shown by this chart available on Seeking Alpha.

Now, there are reasons to expect a manufacturing activity rebound, both because of the long recession we are going through and because of government spending.

In my case, I have hand-picked a few of the best industrials and have particularly focused on machinery manufacturers such as Deere (DE) or Caterpillar (CAT). I have been a shareholder of the former but sold it last year on an alluring after-earnings price pop. Recently, I shared a comparison between Caterpillar and Deere to understand which one could be the best pick amidst a tariff-determined environment.

I am also exposed to the ground transportation sector, working on Class 1 railroads such as Norfolk Southern (NSC) and Canadian National (CNI). Perhaps the latest and most interesting addition to my portfolio was United Rentals (URI). It is the leading equipment rental company in the U.S., and it runs a business that is both protected from tariffs and exposed to the tailwind of rising second-hand equipment prices (URI sells its equipment after a few years). Although I stink at market timing, my mid-April buy has turned out to be quite good, and the stock is up 10%.

Now, from the YtD heat map, my picks among industrials are not the sector-leading ones: railroads are down by a few percentage points, and rentals are mixed. Farm and heavy construction is seeing strength in farming equipment, while companies such as Caterpillar (CAT) and Paccar (PCAR) are coming down from their peaks (perhaps new opportunities are opening up). On the other hand, aerospace and defense are mostly in the green, driving the sector up.

Now, there might be some who prefer to invest in the whole sector without cherry-picking a few stocks. In this case, several ETFs track the industry in different ways the industry.

I recently reviewed the Industrial Sector SPDR Fund (NYSEARCA: XLI), but there is another option, which is the Vanguard Industrials Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VIS). The latter has 367 holdings, while XLI only has 78. Moreover, XLI is more concentrated in its top holdings, while VIS spreads the weights of its stocks more evenly.

For example, XLI sees aerospace and defense with a 24.5% weight, while VIS shows a 20.5% weight.

Their top ten holdings are the same, even though the weights are different. For example, XLI has Uber (UBER) as its second-largest holding, while in VIS's case, we have it as number 5 with a 2.66% weight.

Stock XLI Weight VIS Weight
GE Aerospace 5.42% 4.17%
Uber Technologies 4.31% 2.66%
RTX Corp 4.14% 3.39%
Caterpillar Inc 3.73% 3.06%
Boeing Co 3.38% 2.07%
Honeywell Intl. 3.37% 2.65%
Union Pacific Corp 3.16% 2.75%
Automatic Data Processing 2.99% 2.39%
Deere & Co 2.95% 2.34%
Eaton Corp PLC 2.85% 2.07%

When we consider the dividend yield, VIS offers a 1.3% while XLI is a bit higher with 1.44%. At the same time, the 5-yr dividend growth CAGR is higher for VIS: 5.04% vs. 4.44%.

VIS also has lower liquidity, with an AUM of $5.34B (XLI: $19.36B) and an average daily share volume that is around 120k (XLI: 11M). As a result, the average daily dollar volume for VIS is around $30M, while XLI is close to $1.5B.

Like every Vanguard fund, its expense ratio is extremely competitive at 0.09%. Similar ETFs have way higher costs.

As we can see from the ETF grades Seeking Alpha awards, the two ETFs are more or less balanced: VIS has lower risk but also lower liquidity and a slightly weaker momentum; XLI does better in expenses and liquidity.

I think the choice between the two comes from how each one of us views the industry and wants to gain exposure to it, considering the tailwinds that many are waiting to appear. For those who believe that aerospace and defense will have the lion's share of the growth, then XLI is surely a better pick. It also has the nice addition of having Uber as the second-largest holding, offering exposure to a capital-light business.

Those who believe that the whole industry will benefit from government spending and reshoring activity might choose VIS, giving preference also to a slightly faster dividend growth pace.

Therefore, I consider both VIS and XLI interesting buys, with the addition that VIS could soon test its ATHs and perhaps offer a breakout if manufacturing activity rebounds strongly in the next few quarters.

data.title: Lucid: Another Revenue Miss
data.description:
After the bell on Tuesday, we received first quarter results from Lucid (NASDAQ:LCID). The luxury electric vehicle maker has been one of the most disappointing companies in the space in recent years, failing to meet its long-term growth targets. While the company reiterated its full-year production guidance this week, the financials here remain extremely ugly, keeping shares close to their all-time public trading low.

Previous Coverage of the Name

When I last looked at Lucid back in late February, the company announced its fourth quarter results, along with the surprise resignation of co-founder Peter Rawlinson. At that time, revenues beat significantly reduced street estimates, but large losses and cash burn continued. As the company was early in the midst of its Gravity SUV production ramp, investors also had to digest the news of a new interim leader.

My sell rating on this stock has been in place since shares were over $18 back in the summer of 2022, and the stock has lost almost all of its value since then. Lucid shares have actually risen a little since my previous report, a gain of more than 2%, outpacing the S&P's loss of about 4%, but it is a drop in the bucket compared to the losses investors have seen over time.

The Q1 2025 Report

For the quarter, Lucid reported revenues of $235 million. While this number was up 36% over the prior year period, it did miss street estimates by more than $11 million. This ended a streak of three consecutive top line beats, after Lucid had mostly been known for missing street revenue expectations. Lucid delivered over 3,100 vehicles in the quarter, a more than 58% increase as compared to Q1 2024.

The main problem for Lucid is that its low volumes have led to dramatic operating losses. Gross margins in the period were negative 97%, and while this was an improvement of about 37 percentage points over the prior year period, that is definitely not a sustainable business model. Operations lost over $691 million in the quarter, nearly three times the amount of revenue generated. Investors are hoping that as the Gravity ramp leads to more delivery records, losses will decline, but Lucid is not projected to see any kind of profitability for some time.

These large losses have led to significant cash burn over time. Lucid burned through about $590 million in Q1, although this was an improvement from the $715 million figure seen a year earlier. The company ended Q1 with a little more than $4.5 billion in cash and investments against about $2 billion in total debt. Lucid ended the quarter with approximately $5.76 billion in total liquidity, but it may look to raise additional funds to help with the Gravity production ramp as well as the launch of a more affordable vehicle in 2026.

Lucid finished Q1 with about 3.05 billion shares outstanding, up significantly from the more than 1.83 billion figure seen just two years earlier. We've seen numerous debt and equity offerings to keep the name afloat in recent years, with a lot of help coming from the Saudi Public Investment Fund ("PIF"). The PIF currently owns a majority of Lucid shares.

2025 Growth Expectations

When Lucid went public through a special purpose acquisition company ("SPAC") back in 2021, there were hopes for tremendous growth. At that time, management thought it would produce 20,000 Air Sedans in 2022, and by 2025, production of at least three vehicles (including the Gravity) would total 135,000 units. As we've seen with most electric vehicle startups, dramatic growth has been hard to come by. The Gravity was eventually delayed, and Lucid's third model is now a 2026 story.

After producing just over 9,000 vehicles last year, the company originally called for 20,000 units of production this year. With trade war concerns sparking fears of global economic slowdowns, there were fears that this forecast would come down, but management did reiterate it on Tuesday. However, revenue estimates have still dropped to a new low, as seen below, as analysts do remain at least a little skeptical.

I will be curious to see as this year progresses if Lucid breaks down its production and or delivery figures by model. This would allow us to see how the Gravity ramp is going, but also if Lucid is able to grow unit sales of the Air Sedan in a material way. Like many EVs out there, the Air has seen its share of promotions to boost sales volumes, and further incentives would provide an added headwind to the margin progress story.

A Look at Current Valuation

While Lucid is expected to show some strong revenue percentage growth in the coming years, it also remains a very expensive stock currently. As the chart below shows, only Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) has a price-to-sales ratio, based on current respective 2025 analyst estimates, that is higher than Lucid. The other names in this space I've used as a comparison are VinFast Auto Ltd. (VFS), Rivian Automotive, Inc. (RIVN), XPeng Inc. (XPEV), BYD Company Limited (OTCPK:BYDDF), NIO Inc. (NIO), and Polestar Automotive Holding UK PLC (PSNY). These numbers were as of Wednesday's close.

If you average the 7 names other than Lucid, the result is about 2.67 times each's expected 2025 revenue. Lucid finished Wednesday going for about 5.10 times, a significant premium to this comparison group. If we were to exclude Tesla, however, that average drops to just 1.61 times, and these numbers don't include traditional US automakers like Ford Motor Company (F) or General Motors Company (GM) that trade at about 0.25 times their expected revenues for this year. The average Lucid price target as of Wednesday night was $2.53, implying low double-digit upside, but that number had been dramatically cut from almost $37 just three years ago.

There remain two potential major risks for those betting against the name. The first one is that the Saudis just take this company private, buying out the rest of Lucid that they don't already own. The second risk here is that there's a meaningful improvement in the company's financial results in 2025, once the Gravity really starts to see meaningful deliveries, sparking some enthusiasm for the mid-size platform expected to arrive in 2026.

Final Thoughts and Recommendation

In the end, Lucid announced mixed results on Tuesday. The company did make some progress on the margin front, but it did miss revenue estimates, and it is still losing tons of money. With cash burn likely to continue for some time, another capital raise is not out of the question, and the valuation remains quite a bit inflated compared to many peers in the space.

While this should be a strong year for delivery and revenue growth in percentage terms, Lucid remains a sell in my opinion. The luxury electric vehicle space is very small, and Lucid hasn't shown that it can hit its growth targets or meaningfully improve its financials. Perhaps the mid-size platform launch will get me to eventually change my rating, but that's still about a year out, and Lucid has never proven it can produce anything in sizable volumes to this point. Should management eventually cut yearly guidance due to global economic pressures, a new low for shares would not surprise me.

data.title: Flutter Entertainment Q1: FanDuel Takes Decisive Lead In IGaming
data.description:
Flutter Entertainment plc (NYSE:FLUT) reported the company’s Q1 results on May 7th after market close. While earnings missed expectations with slightly adverse U.S. sports results and low international growth, Flutter’s underlying performance remains strong. Going forward, the company sees significant earnings growth ahead from U.S. maturation as well as acquisitions of Snai and NSX.

In my previous January article on the stock, “Flutter: FanDuel's Battle With DraftKings Continues,” I up kept a Hold rating based on FanDuel’s great momentum but a corresponding valuation. The stock has since lost -7% of its value, losing by a sliver to the S&P 500’s (SP500) decline of -4%.

Flutter’s Q1 Was Fair Against Ambitious Expectations, Highlights iGaming Lead

Flutter’s Q1 report continued the company’s momentum in the U.S. through FanDuel. The market’s revenues reached $1666 million, up 18% year-on-year. Average monthly players grew by 10.6%, sportsbook revenues grew by 15.0%, and iGaming revenues grew the best at a 31.8% increase year-on-year, showing continued momentum as the U.S. market continues to grow in size. The revenue growth was achieved in spite of a 70 basis point adverse impact from realized sports results year-on-year.

The quarter reflects a continued U.S. rivalry between FanDuel and DraftKings (DKNG). The brands’ sports betting market shares have remained very close to each other in early 2025, whereas Flutter’s especially rapid revenue growth in iGaming clearly shows FanDuel’s rapid market share gains against DraftKings in recent months. FanDuel’s iGaming strategy, revolving around site-wide jackpots, exclusive content, and a referral system for customer acquisition, has clearly succeeded as the quarter highlights. While FanDuel's and DraftKings' iGaming market shares were hand-in-hand in the latter parts of 2024, a gap has surfaced to 28.0% and 23.5% market shares, respectively. iGaming is still a smaller market than sports betting in the U.S., but FanDuel's successful efforts could still become significantly valuable.

As US operations continue to scale and the competitive environment is largely dominated by the two giants, Flutter’s U.S. operations have continued to achieve significant margin expansion. Adjusted EBITDA for the market reached $161 million at a margin of 9.7%, up a massive 790 basis points year-on-year. The clear majority of margin expansion was achieved through a 750 basis point reduction in marketing expenses, as competition is easing. Operating leverage also contributed to better margins. As the number of already active FanDuel users gradually increases, Flutter’s U.S. earnings are set to continue scaling rapidly.

Flutter’s international operations performed stably, not achieving significant growth. Revenues reached $1999 million, up 3% year-on-year in constant currency. While the SEA region performed well with 13.7% growth, weakness in the Asia Pacific region offset most of Flutter’s international growth. Adjusted EBITDA for international operations grew by 2% in constant currencies to $518 million. The results underline stable operations, as most of Flutter’s international markets are quite mature.

Ultimately, the quarter’s total revenues of $3.67 billion missed Wall Street’s consensus by $150 million, and the adjusted EPS of $1.59 missed by $0.30. Adverse U.S. sports results caused some weakness compared to Wall Street’s ambitious estimates, whereas the underlying performance seems in line with expectations.

Going forward, Flutter adjusted the 2025 guidance based on sports results, currency fluctuations, and acquisitions, while the organic outlook has stayed similar. Revenues are expected by Flutter to scale to $17080 million with growth of 21.6%, and adjusted EBITDA is expected to reach $3180 million with 34.9% growth as US profitability continues to scale.

Snai, NSX Acquisitions Bolster Flutter Internationally

Flutter has been active in the M&A market to bolster the company’s position internationally. The company previously announced the acquisition of Snai in September for €2.3 billion, adding nearly 10% in market share to Flutter’s online operations in Italy to a total of nearly 30%. The acquisition was recently completed in late April after regulatory approval, boosting earnings, with further benefits to come from integrating Flutter’s pricing and other capabilities with Snai.

The company also expects to close the announced acquisition of NSX in May, receiving a 56% stake in NSX for around $350 million. NSX is the 4th largest operator in Brazil, positioning Flutter in an attractive growth market—Brazilian sports betting and iGaming are projected to scale rapidly after regulatory changes. Flutter is able to leverage its technology with a local operator to gain market share in a potentially significant market.

While relatively small in size compared to Flutter’s massive global operations, the acquisitions underline Flutter’s capability to grow its market position with significant technological and cost synergies.

Updated Valuation

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Flutter’s stock. I added $2950 million worth of debt to Flutter’s balance sheet to account for the acquisitions of Snai and NSX.

As Flutter’s growth in the U.S. continues, and the company integrates acquisitions, the company’s growth prospects remain great. I estimate a 2024-2034 revenue CAGR of 9.8% with 2% perpetual growth afterwards, raising my expectations moderately from previous ones. I still expect the company to ultimately reach a 26% adjusted EBITDA margin, and that Flutter’s free cash flow remains fairly good.

The estimates put Flutter’s fair value at $266.55, 10% above the stock price at the time of writing; the stock is priced with a modest upside, representing a fair margin of safety.

The fair value estimate is up from $247.02 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital (OTC:WACC) of 10.25%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 6.04% interest rate, annualized Q1 interest expenses, and debt. I again estimate a moderate 15% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond (US10Y) yield of 4.30% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.18. With an ESG add-on of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 11.11% and the WACC at 10.25%.

Takeaway

Flutter’s Q1 continued FanDuel’s success in spite of more unfavorable sports results, reflecting the brand’s recent market share gains in iGaming as well as rapid margin expansion from declining marketing expenses as FanDuel and DraftKings are cementing themselves as the two industry leaders. Flutter’s international performance was stable. The company has great growth prospects ahead with the U.S. market as well as through the acquisitions of Snai and NSX. As Flutter’s stock is valued with a fair margin of safety, I remain with a Hold rating for FLUT.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CECO Environmental: Awaiting Margin Improvements
data.description:
In October, I believed that shares of CECO Environmental (NASDAQ:CECO) were seeing interesting times as expectations were on the rise. Momentum was unleashed after a recalibrated strategy, yet I was a bit cautious as earnings growth was lagging while expectations were on the rise.

After a soft end to 2024, the company started 2025 on a strong note, at least in terms of sales, as margins remain volatile and lagging here.

Protecting People, Environment, Equipment And Processes

The paragraph header is the mission of CECO Environmental, founded back in 1966. The company generates over half a billion in sales split between three end markets, which all focus on industrial areas. This includes energy transition, industrial water and industrial air.

Specific sub-segments and applications include functions like emission management, thermal & acoustics management, gas & liquid separation, carbon capture, supply water treatment, waste water treatment, scrubbers, odor management, dust removal, among others.

A focus on the reindustrialization of the US amidst re-shoring, built-out of infrastructure, renewable investments, as well as more stringent environment regulations have benefited the business which has grown to employ some 1,500 workers over time.

Listed since the 1980s, shares have seen a real volatile past performance, as a $5 stock in 2020 was quite depressed. The company has undergone a real transition, supported by sound bolt-on deals and focus on higher margin activities to improve the business, seen in growing orders and an impressive performance in 2023 and 2024.

An Impressive Improvement

The real growth started in 2022, a year in which revenues rose by 30% to $422 million, with orders up 46% to $526 million, allowing adjusted earnings to essentially triple to $0.74 per share. While strategic actions, an economic recovery and acquisitions were explanations for growth, it felt that some of the growth came as a surprise.

2023 sales rose another 29% to $545 million, although that an 11% increase in the backlog to $582 million was a bit less impressive, with earnings flattish at $0.75 per share.

The company guided for 2024 sales to advance to $587 million. Trading at $25 per share, the 36 million shares of the business were valued at $900 million and including about $100 million in net debt, the business was valued at a billion, at less than 2 times sales and about 15 times EBITDA. However, the company outlined a convincing 2025 guidance, with sales seen up to $700-$750 million, with EBITDA seen up to $95 million.

A $125 million cash deal to acquire Profire Energy, announced in the fall of last year, was set to drive the anticipated growth here, adding technology for industrial combustion applications, and about $60 million in annual sales.

Pro forma net debt would jump to $200 million, equal to about 2 times EBITDA based on the 2025 guidance. With earnings power still seen around $0.80-$0.90 per share, I was a bit cautious as shares traded at 30 times earnings, as I was mindful of the share price performance in recent years as well, making me a bit reserved, yet interested to watch the story unfold.

Expectations Move Around

After shares traded in the mid-twenties in October, shares saw a big rally towards year-end, with shares hitting highs around the $35 mark in December. This was followed by a big decline to the $17 mark amidst the rout caused by the tariff war, as shares have recovered to $25 per share at the moment of writing.

In the meantime, there have been quite some corporate events. This started this year, as the company closed the purchase of Profire early in January. By the middle of the month, the company posted soft preliminary fourth quarter results (and thereby full year 2024 results) as the company announced the closing of the acquisition of Verantis in December, a deal adding $45 million in annual sales. At the same time, the company announced its intention to divest its Fluid Handling businesses.

In February, the company posted a mere 2% increase in full-year sales to $558 million, although that the backlog showed real growth with full-year orders up 14% to $667 million. Adjusted EBITDA was up 9% to nearly $63 million, as adjusted earnings actually were down two pennies to $0.73 per share. GAAP earnings only came in at half that number and while much of the discrepancy is due to amortization charges, there are some cash expenses involved in the reconciliation as well.

The company reiterated its 2025 guidance with sales seen at $700-$750 million, with EBITDA seen at $90-$100 million. This should be seen in light of the softer 2024 results, but comes after another deal (Verantis) has been announced as well.

On the first day of April, the company announced the divestment of the fluids handling business to May River Capital in a deal valued at $110 million. The first quarter earnings report revealed at $65 million gain on that sale.

The first quarter has been very strong with first quarter sales up 40% to $177 million as orders were up as much as 57% to $228 million, adding to a backlog which now exceeds six hundred million. While growth was impressive, adjusted operating income was down 16% to just $8.6 million, with adjusted earnings down a penny to $0.10 per share. The divergent paths with regard to margins were attributed to strategic actions taken to manage the backlog and pull in inventory purchases amidst tariffs, set to pay off in the coming quarters.

Net debt was reported at $194 million amidst the sale of the fluids handling business and the purchase of Profire, as cash flows in the first quarter were on the soft side. First quarter adjusted EBITDA of $14 million felt soft, as the company maintained the full-year guidance at $90-$100 million on this front.

What Now?

With 37 million shares trading at $25 and net debt seen at $194 million, the company is valued at $1.1 billion. Of course, based on sales topping $700 million this year, sales multiples come in around 1.5 times, yet the spectacular sequential improvements are seen in EBITDA should reduce those multiples to the low-teens.

Of course, adjusted earnings should likely see real improvement, as earnings could top a dollar per share, but the company first has to deliver on this amidst many moving parts (through M&A on both the buy and sell side) as well as margin volatility within the business. This is in part driven by tariffs, seen having a gross impact between $3 and $10 million this year, ahead of mitigating factors.

Maintaining the full-year sales guidance looks a bit conservative, given recent M&A action and the strong first quarter sales numbers, but the risks are clearly in the margins here, after first quarter margins were depressed.

All this means that the business is showing some real momentum, but mostly in terms of orders and sales growth, and while margins are anticipated to improve, that was not seen in the first quarter. While I believe that the company could deliver on better results and post earnings around a dollar per share, I am still performing the same balancing act as the fall.

Right now, I require a better entry point in the teens and/or improvement in the margins before considering this neutral stance.

data.title: BeiGene, Ltd., Soon To Be BeOne Medicines: New Name And New Profit
data.description:
Company Background

BeiGene, Ltd. (NASDAQ:ONC), according to its latest 10-Q report, describes itself as a

a leading global oncology company discovering and developing innovative treatments that are more accessible and affordable to cancer patients worldwide.

It develops and commercializes innovative drugs for the treatment of cancer, particularly chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), which has a global diagnosis rate of approximately 191,000 each year, of which approximately 23,000 are in the U.S. alone. The company was founded in 2010 (as BeiGene) by John Oyler, an American entrepreneur, who continues to serve as its CEO, and Xiaodong Wang, a Chinese biochemist, who continues to serve as Chairman of its Scientific Advisory Board.

The company has an interesting history, which is worth detailing briefly before I go on to examine its main products and assess their recent earnings. I should note that the company has announced its intent to rebrand itself from BeiGene to BeOne Medicines Ltd., having announced its intention to do so in late 2024. To properly acknowledge this change, when discussing its present and future, I will refer to it as BeOne, but will acknowledge its other name when discussing its history or past results. For the purposes of understanding "the company," the names are essentially interchangeable.

History

As explained in an insightful interview with Life Science Leader, John Oyler’s belief in the future potential of Chinese biotechnology, coupled with his background as a McKinsey consultant who frequently worked in China, led him to meet and subsequently work with Wang to found BeiGene (I assume as a play on “Beijing”). Wang was considered something of a "rock star" among the Chinese biotech community, and figured centrally as a charismatic leader who could attract top Chinese talent that might otherwise have left for more lucrative opportunities in the US or Europe. Oyler and Wang set out to make BeiGene a leader in this space as Chinese biotech companies found their feet internationally, believing that funding opportunities would follow as Chinese medical research blossomed as part of the country’s rapid modernization.

They established the company to embed Chinese biotech among the world’s best, and they soon attracted outside investment. This not only helped fund early operations (labs and manufacturing capabilities) but also added credibility to their burgeoning reputation and validated their focus on developing a multinational biotech company focused on oncology, with Chinese roots. The first outside investment came from Merck (MRK) in 2011, valued at $20 million, in exchange for some licensing agreements in their pre-clinical cancer research. This helped them expand their presence within and then beyond China.

Though now officially headquartered in Cambridge, MA, Oyler and Wang were focused on giving the company a truly multinational presence and have established offices in 26 different countries across six continents. This has set them apart from other biotech companies that have either focused on centering themselves in one particular geographical area or have established subsidiaries in other locales. Oyler and Wang wanted to avoid that as they recognized a potential advantage from spreading themselves out. As Oyler explained in the interview mentioned earlier, neither wanted BeiGene to be a primarily Beijing or U.S.-based company, for example, by creating a subsidiary in the other location, “because the second you do that, you’re belittling somebody,” Oyler argued.

He and Wang believed that their truly multinational presence has helped to create a company that is both "transformational to the industry — and lasting," according to the article’s author, Rob Wright. They also believed that establishing a strong presence in China, especially, would accelerate the trial enrolment process, as Wright explained:

They believed it possible to run high-quality clinical trials in China that were global in nature and design, so they weren’t managing different clinical trial systems (i.e., one for China and one for the rest of the world). In doing so, they could enroll patients more rapidly and run trials more quickly, providing for a lower cost basis.

In time, they developed a system to cut down the trial enrolment time by a significant margin, which not only expedited the processes of drug discovery and approval but also saved money. The relentless pursuit of time- and cost-saving measures, which they have been able to apply across the globe, gave them a competitive advantage.

Focus and Strategy

The interview in which Oyler outlined this strategy was from 2019, and I was encouraged to hear that management has maintained the same focus going forward, as outlined in their latest earnings call. Oyler commented:

Our internal clinical development team of 3,700-plus continues to demonstrate time, cost, and quality advantages that are driving higher ROI for each R&D dollar spent. And we’re routinely seeing proof of these advantages and programs in competitive settings.

This quote was accompanied by the graphic below from the earnings presentation:

Throughout its history, BeiGene has stayed true to its global focus and sought out opportunities to partner with other companies to maximize growth and commercial opportunities. As such, they have been able to exploit the opportunities their deep roots in China have afforded, for example, through licensing agreements with other biotech firms. In 2020, Amgen (AMGN) invested $2.8 billion in BeiGene, essentially purchasing 20.5% of the company’s shares. The investment allowed Amgen to sell three of its key products (Xgeva, Blincyto, and Kyprolis) in China, for which BeiGene generates a portion of the revenue. This amounted to a total of $114 million in Q1 2025, which is not bad at all (about 10% of its revenue). Part of the agreement also included offering a board seat for Amgen, which was filled (and remains so) by Anthony Hopper, the former Executive VP of Global Commercial Operations at Amgen.

BeiGene’s growing footprint in oncology has been impressive, and this is reflected in the company’s rising share price. Since their IPO in 2016, they have handily beaten the S&P 500 (in orange), as seen below, but they have fallen 35.4% in the 3 and a half years since their all-time high in October 2021, against the SPY’s 31% gain over the same period. That said, shares have risen almost 45% in the last twelve months. It’s been a rollercoaster ride for investors, but – unless you invested at any point during 2021 – not a terrible one.

BeOne’s revenue growth has been impressive, increasing 60% in its largest market (U.S., approximating 50% of its revenue mix) and 37.45% across the three other regions.

While previous growth is great to see, investors today will want to know whether this can be sustained going forward, and if (and perhaps how quickly) the company can recover to make a new all-time high. I will look at the scope of their approved medicines, the potential for their drug pipeline, and consider their financials in the context of their most recent results, released on Wednesday.

BeOne’s Current Offerings

BeOne currently has two approved medicines, which I’ll discuss in turn: Brukinsa, a Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitor that targets various forms of blood cancer (Leukemia), and Tevimbra, a checkpoint inhibitor that targets solid tumors and blood cancers. Tevimbra received its FDA approval in March 2024 – to treat advanced or metastatic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC) after prior chemotherapy – while Brukinsa was approved in the US in 2019 for the treatment of Mantle Cell Lymphoma, but has had four further approvals since, to treat Relapsed or Refractory Marginal Zone Lymphoma (2021), Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (2023), Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (2024), and Relapsed or Refractory Follicular Lymphoma (2025).

Brukinsa: A Revenue Generating Machine

Brukinsa generated $791.6 million in revenue (globally) in the most recent quarter, up from $488.5 million in Q1 2024, representing an increase of 62% year over year. In their latest earnings, management remarked that Brukinsa had reached a milestone of treating over 200,000 patients worldwide, and demand has accelerated rapidly quarter-over-quarter. Much of this increase was derived from US sales, which totaled $563 million for the quarter.

Over the years, the progress made through these approvals has been reflected very positively in financial terms and in a clinical sense. Latterly, research from the New England Journal of Medicine has proved that Brukinsa (Zanubrutinib) is more effective than a key competitor, Ibrutinib, which is produced collaboratively by Pharmacyclics/AbbVie (ABBV) and Janssen/Johnson & Johnson (JNJ); it showed a higher response rate, improved progression-free survival score, and a better safety profile. It also has a competitive edge over its other main rival, Acalabrutinib, produced by AstraZeneca (AZN); Brukinsa offers a similar efficacy but better safety profile. According to a study in the academic journal, Blood,

Zanubrutinib was associated with a 27% lower risk of major cardiovascular adverse events and a 51% lower risk of new-onset atrial fibrillation/flutter compared to acalabrutinib.

From a financial standpoint, Brukinsa is also stealing market share from these competitors. The total revenue derived in Q1 2025 from this group of medications was approximately $1.6 billion, of which Brukinsa earned a portion approximating 35%. However, not only did it generate more revenue, but it also showed superior revenue growth compared to its peers.

What is clearly happening is that physicians and patients have begun to switch their preferences toward Brukinsa, which opens up the runway for future revenue generation. Should they grab even half of the remaining 65% of the market currently being occupied by their competitors—and at 60% annual revenue growth, this won’t take long—this will represent more than half a billion in added revenue per quarter. That translates to $2 billion per year. This milestone could be reached within two or three years, in my estimation.

Also showing promise is their rapid approval progress, as seen in the map below. Brukinsa is currently approved in 75 markets, and new and expanded reimbursement agreements are being signed regularly (11 in the last quarter alone). They are currently seeking approval for Brukinsa in India, Colombia, Algeria, Morocco, Vietnam, and Turkey, countries that collectively represent – according to the latest data – almost 22% of the world’s population. Successful approvals – and we have no reason to bet against that, given their track record with other countries—would give access to another 1.8 billion people. This may take a year or so and depend upon reimbursement agreements being signed, but it represents a phenomenal opportunity for expansion.

All in all, the potential for Brukinsa to continue growing is very positive. The drug’s patent is protected until at least 2034, while for some conditions the protection is even longer. BeOne had some recent success in legal patent disputes in this regard, firstly from MSN Pharmaceuticals in 2024, and more recently, in 2025, from Pharmacyclics—they won in both cases.

Tevimbra: A Solid Wingman with Potential

While Brukinsa represented 71.4% of BeOne’s revenue in Q1 2025 – up from 65.5% in Q1 2024 – and is clearly the company’s leading drug in terms of both present and future revenue generation, Tevimbra is a solid back-up, with further progress forthcoming in terms of trials – to expand its treatment targets – and regulatory approvals.

On top of its prior FDA approval for treating ESCC in 2024, it has also received further approval in the U.S. for gastric cancer, alongside European approval to treat advanced/metastatic ESCC and gastric or gastroesophageal junction cancer and non-small cell lung cancer (2024). Speaking about its U.S. potential in the latest call, the CEO, John Oyler, remarked:

We're very encouraged with the progress that's been made on Tevimbra in the US. We were very pleased to receive our frontline esophageal approval. That was our third approval in the US within a year, coming after second-line esophageal and frontline gastric, and, of course, we were also very pleased to see listing in the NCCN [National Comprehensive Cancer Network] guidelines. And also more recently, our alternative dosing Q2W and Q4W came in line as well. So what we now see is initial experiences, patient starts, all in line with launch expectations. And we'll see the second quarter really be the starting point for Tevimbra in the U.S. now that all those other factors have come into line.

It is difficult to gauge potential peak revenue generation for Tevimbra, but with the drug seemingly near the start of its journey through the approval processes internationally, and with further treatment options available, it shows promise. Approval is currently being sought for Tevimbra to treat nasopharyngeal cancer (in the EU), non-small cell lung cancer (in the EU), and gastric cancer (in Japan), with news expected in the back half of 2025 or first half of 2026.

Tevimbra saw revenue of $171.2 million in Q1 2025, up from $145.3 million year-over-year, representing a decent 17.8% growth, but it is unclear if this can be sustained, mainly because of intense competition. Tevimbra competes directly against Merck’s blockbuster drug Keytruda, which generated $7.21 billion in the latest quarter, and also Imfinzi (AZN), Opdivo (BMY), and Ivonescimab, developed jointly by Akeso Inc. (OTCPK:AKESF) and Summit Therapeutics (SMMT). Just recently, research revealed the latter superior to Tevimbra in a research trial. It is a crowded space, and while Tevimbra, upon its launch in October 2024, was priced slightly cheaper than Keytruda for competitive purposes, the commercial success of this drug on a long-term basis is by no means guaranteed, particularly given Keytruda’s strong position and brand awareness. Unless best-in-class, they risk becoming redundant over time. That’s why having a large and diversified pipeline can help with these types of cases.

Drug Pipeline: Hematology and Beyond

By all accounts, BeOne’s pipeline is large and expansive, covering hematology (the study of blood) and solid tumors (think: breast cancer, lung cancer, etc.). The company is currently undergoing trials involving Brukinsa either on its own or in combination with another drug in its collection, Sonrotoclax, for the treatment of CLL. Sonrotoclax is a BCL-2 inhibitor that has the potential to treat a range of B-cell malignancies. Early data suggests it could become best-in-class and is currently being evaluated for approval in China ahead of global approval (including FDA and EMA) being sought in the back half of 2025. It is also being trialed against its chief rival, Venetoclax (made by AbbVie), to test for comparative efficacy, with a read-out scheduled for later in the year. Management is confident in its superiority.

BeOne is also trialing a BTK-targeted Chimeric Degradation Activation Compound (CDAC) for the treatment of CLL and other B-cell malignancies. This offers a great deal of promise, potentially as the first-in-class and the best-in-class treatment that can surpass existing options for treating CLL. They have dosed 600 patients so far and have plans to conduct a separate experiment pitching their version against Eli Lilly’s (LLY) Jaypirca (pirtobrutinib) in the back half of 2025; so far, separate studies identified superior efficacy for BeOne’s treatment, as shown below. I share management’s confidence that the results will confirm its superiority.

Outside of these late-stage trials in hematology, where BeOne has become a (newly) recognized leader, the company has numerous early-stage trials planned or currently underway targeting several areas in solid tumors, including lung, breast/gynecologic, gastrointestinal, and pan-tumor.

As a non-expert in this line of research, my only thought is that if they can afford to conduct these studies, and have found a way to advance their research at even a slightly quicker pace than their competition, they are giving themselves every chance of finding something remarkable that can be built upon. It stands to reason that the more trials set up, and the quicker those trials can be conducted, the greater the chance that one or more of them will show clinically meaningful data that can translate to drug approvals and, eventually, revenue.

As such, I view these trials and the expectation for data read-outs throughout 2025 and 2026 as potentially positive catalysts for the company. As early-stage proof-of-concept trials, some of these will move on to the next phase, and some will fail. Investors should expect mixed results, which are entirely normal. The best management teams learn from them.

Company Financials and Valuation Assessment

BeOne has $2.516 billion in cash and equivalents. It has assets totaling $5.842 billion and liabilities of $2.342 billion, including a total debt of $996.8 million. Its quick ratio stands at 1.63, and its current ratio is 1.96, which is fine. While it burned through $12 million in Q1 2025, its FCF has improved markedly over the past two years, as seen in its rapidly declining trailing twelve months FCF (below).

Its operating cash flow for the quarter was $44 million, while its cap-ex spending of $56 million showed a meaningful reduction from $112 million in Q4 2024 and $155 million in Q3 2024.

Revenue growth has been solid and, since Q2 2022 (previous to which it faced tough comparisons from the revenue jump in Q1 2021), it has slowed from 127.7% to 48.6%, which is not terrible by any means. Most importantly, the trend line is pointing in the right direction.

Quarter-over-quarter, their revenue has increased faster than their operating expenses, as they have successfully scaled the business through this period of rapid growth toward profitability. Indeed, the company recorded its first quarter of positive net income ($1.2 million) in Q1 2025. Their operating margin has improved quarter over quarter, and they are on their way toward enjoying their first full year of profitability in 2025. R&D expenses have crept up quarter over quarter, which is good to see, averaging most recently around $500 million, but their cost-saving measures have kept these in check too. They are trying to strike a balance, and I believe they are succeeding. Again, their improved operating margins tell the story (below).

Management has estimated the maintenance of their gross margin in the mid-80s and guided to between $4.9 and 5.3 billion in revenue for the full year 2025, both of which are fantastic. Based on their quarterly growth rates, however, and what I anticipate in terms of them stealing market share with Brukinsa, I actually view this revenue estimate as conservative, and would not be surprised if they revise this figure upwards or, at the very least, meet the high end of this guidance. The company is firing on all cylinders, and I expect further growth ahead.

Stock-based compensation represented $95 million in the last quarter, representing just 8.5% of their revenue ($1.117 billion), which is absolutely fair. Share dilution has been minimal over the past three years, and I do not expect any material dilution going forward, especially as they will likely register positive FCF throughout the rest of 2025, as revenues increase. They have ample cash.

I like to invest in companies with strong insider ownership, as this helps keep management and shareholders' goals aligned. While insider ownership was just 0.78%, I was pleased that co-founder Xiaodong Wang was the larger insider owner, at 0.7% shares overall, totalling around $184 million. There have been no insider buys on the open market in the last two years, which is disappointing. Amgen remains the largest institutional holder, though I note their stake has been reduced to 17.55%, likely not due to selling but because the share count has expanded over the years they have held their position. I view the sustained support from such a well-established biotech player as a net positive.

In terms of valuation, it is very difficult to come to an accurate fair value for a company at the early stages of its growth. Looking at P/E or even forward P/E ratio is meaningless for at least a few more years, but from a P/S perspective, at least. Shares look a tad expensive but not insanely priced.

Seeking Alpha gives them a D rating for valuation in comparison to peers, but I notice their 5-year average P/S is 22. Really, what investors will pay a premium for here is their superb revenue growth, but there are some risks attached to that estimation. Again, with so many unknowns in terms of future revenue or FCF over the coming years, attempting to predict earnings growth or FCF generation to discount it back will be overly speculative. Indeed, the lack of any reliable measure of valuation probably accounts for the yo-yoing share price over the past few years, and this might continue for the foreseeable future, at least until the market potential for its two drugs, and the leading others in their late-stage pipeline, are clarified. As such, I expect less volatility going into 2026.

Conclusion

BeOne is clearly a well-managed, founder-led, vision-oriented company at the forefront of cancer research. They are a small player but have the potential to grow and compete with the major biotech companies in time. They have done an excellent job of making themselves relevant in this space and were rewarded this quarter with their first meaningful net profit.

I believe they have the potential to sustain their growth through their competitive advantages, notably their geographical spread that offers, essentially, supply chain efficiencies through faster trial recruitment and completion, and their intellectual property. They have secured solid backing from Amgen, among others, providing both support and modest revenue from licensing agreements in China, and have developed and commercialized two of their own products that have considerable revenue-generating potential. While there is some risk to Tevimbra, which is unlikely in my view to dislodge competitors like Keytruda, I view Brukinsa and its other drugs currently in development as more lucrative opportunities for the company. As their treatments gain traction among physicians and secure regulatory approval in further locations, I see sustained revenue growth ahead—a long runway of growth.

Their pipeline contains several interesting treatment verticals in both hematology and solid tumors, which could come to represent significant revenue generation if even a small number of them prove successful. While the share price is likely to remain volatile as they go through the ebb-and-flow news cycle of trial data read-outs over the next year—they have more than 10 proof-of-concept trials concluding in 2025—I think their varied approach actually helps to lower the investment risk through inherent diversification. CLL and solid tumor numbers are only going to increase among the general population, and treatments are sorely needed. Their approach could offer novel options that may become best-in-class for patients. Early data show a lot of promise, particularly with the BTK-targeted CDAC treatment. As with all biotech companies, their ability to protect their patents will be key, but they have had success in this area and have some protected patents for the next decade.

Given the inherent risk in this type of company, I’d prefer to enter at what I would perceive to be a bargain price, which I view as around $170. At around that price, I’d view BeOne as a strong buy, but at this price ($220-230), it’s a cautious buy at best. I would not be surprised to see further downside ahead, judging by the price action today.

Indeed, the 4% share price drop following their positive Q1 results on Wednesday suggests that investors were bidding up the price in anticipation of something even better. Moreover, insider buying has been nonexistent for the last two years; that is, not a single share purchased on the open market. These factors together imply overvaluation in my view. As such, I do not believe a share price above $200 offers a compelling risk-reward profile. For long-term investors, however, I believe their patience through the inevitable ups and downs will be rewarded.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kenvue Inc. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Kenvue Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Energy Transfer: Clear Path To Double Digits Return - Tariffs Pose Minimal Headwinds
data.description:
ET Is An Even Better Buy After The Steep Correction, As Power Generation Demand Remains Robust & Tariffs Impose Minimal Impacts

We previously covered Energy Transfer LP (NYSE:ET) in March 2025, discussing why the ongoing correction had triggered the richer forward distribution yields, driven by the robust FY2024 adj EBITDA growth and the rich FY2025 guidance.

Combined with the aggressive capex investments in pipelines, processing plants, and data centers, with it well supporting its double digits upside potential, we had reiterated our Buy rating upon a further pullback to the prior support ranges of $16.50 and $15.20.

ET 1Y Stock Price

Since then, the worst of the tariff induced meltdown has already triggered the drastic retracement to our recommended Buy point of $15 ranges, as the bulls also swopped in to defend ET's 2024 support levels, aided by the recent 90 days tariff pause announced on April 09, 2025.

While OPEC+ may have accelerated its output hikes while triggering the moderation in crude oil/natural gas spot prices nearer to pre-pandemic levels, we are not overly concerned indeed, since the industry has been one that is typically cyclical while dependent on the macro events.

If anything, readers must note ET's excellent growth capex efforts, with it already triggering its volume growth on a YoY basis and growing adj EBITDA to $4.1B in FQ1'25 (+5.6% YoY).

This implies that the management's reiterated FY2025 adj EBITDA guidance of $16.3B at the midpoint (+5.2% YoY) does not appear to be overly ambitious, especially since 90% of its bottom-line prospects are tied to fee-based pricing "with limited commodity price exposure."

This is significantly aided by its "high percentage of take or pay contracts helping to reduce impacts from market volatility," while building upon the robust adj EBITDA growth observed in FY2024 to $15.48B (+13% YoY):

Take-or-Pay Contract are written agreements between a buyer and seller that obligate the buyer to pay regardless of whether or not the seller delivers the good or service...

The clause of the Take-or-Pay Contract which stipulates this obligation for the buyer to pay a compensation in the case he does not take the good or service is commonly called the killing clause. (2b1stconsulting)

The same may be observed in ET's Distributable Cash Flow attributable to partners at $2.3B in FQ1'25 (-2.1% YoY) and $8.36B in FY2024 (+10.4% YoY), allowing them to sustain the rich distribution pay-outs ahead.

These expanded bottom-lines already trigger the healthier balance sheet at a net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of 3.65x as well, compared to FY2024 levels of 3.85x and FY2019 levels of 4.90x, significantly aided by its growing partnerships and the well diversified capital funding.

Moving forward, we believe that ET's long-term prospects remain robust, especially given the durability of AI demand and the ongoing data center capex boom, as similarly reiterated by numerous market leaders/hyperscalers, including Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), and Microsoft (MSFT) in the recent earning calls.

The same promising commentaries have been offered by Equinix, Inc. (EQIX), the global retail colocation leader with 21% in 2024 market share, as they also raise their FY2025 revenue and AFFO per share guidance, as Digital Realty Trust, Inc. (DLR) also reports growing data center backlog through 2027.

These developments further underscore why the data center related demand for ET's power generation capabilities are likely to remain robust, as similarly reported by the management in the recent earnings call, with many of its prior discussions for new power plants, data centers, and industrial and onshore manufacturing capacities already reaching advanced levels.

This is significantly aided by its "solid backlog of well contracted growth projects with strong counterparties" over the next five years and the numerous natural gas fired electric generation facilities/pipelines/processing plants expected to commence service in 2025/2026.

These developments are likely to trigger ET's robust prospects no matter the uncertain macroeconomic environment and the volatile commodity spot prices, as the management also rapidly monetizes its intensified capex efforts.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These excellent numbers may have directly contributed to the consensus' raised forward estimates, with the ET expected to report an accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +5.5%/+4.4% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +4.2%/+4.1%, albeit decelerating compared to the 5Y historical growth at a CAGR of +8.8%/+6.7% and 10Y historical growth at +4%/+12.2%, respectively.

ET Valuations

Despite the potentially painful oil/gas industry's normalization period ahead, we believe that ET is likely to remain highly profitable, as guided by the management.

At the same time, the management has guided minimal tariff impacts on its ongoing constructions and future gas imports from China, as the latter country also waives off their "125% tariff on ethane imports from the United States imposed earlier this month."

These reasons are also why ET remains reasonably valued at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 8.03x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 13.03x, nearer to its 1Y mean of 8.29x/13.58x and its 5Y mean of 7.78x/9.11x, respectively, after the much-needed correction from 2025 heights of 9.18x/16.07x.

The pullback has also triggered its compelling valuations compared to its direct pipeline peers, including Dominion Energy (D) at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 12.09x, Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) at 9.53x, Plains All American (PAA) at 8.58x, MPLX LP (MPLX) at 10.13x, and Kinder Morgan (KMI) at 11.29x, respectively, with it offering interested investors with an excellent margin of safety.

So, Is ET Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

ET 5Y Stock Price

For now, ET has already lost -26.2% or the equivalent -$17.93B of its market capitalization from the January 2025 peaks, as the stock also appears to be well-supported at its recent floor of $15s ranges.

Based on the LTM net income per common unit of $1.32 (+21.1% sequentially) and the 1Y P/E non-GAAP mean of 13.03x, the stock is finally trading below our updated fair value estimates of $17.20.

Despite the consensus lowered FY2027 net income per common unit estimates from $1.80 to $1.64, there remains an excellent upside potential of +34.7% to our long-term price target of $21.30 as well, thanks to the deep sell-off.

Combined with ET's richer forward distribution yields of 8.09%, compared to its 4Y historical averages of 7.42% and the sector median of 5.19%, the moderating short interest volumes by -41% from recent heights, and the bullish support at the recent floor of $15s ranges, we are reiterating our Buy rating here.

data.title: Invesco EQV European Equity Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance quoted is past performance and cannot guarantee comparable future results; current performance may be lower or higher. Visit invesco.com for the most recent month-end performance. Performance figures reflect reinvested distributions and changes in net asset value (NAV). Investment return and principal value will vary so that you may have a gain or a loss when you sell shares. Returns less than one year are cumulative; all others are annualized. Performance shown prior to the inception date of Class R6 shares is that of Class A shares and includes the 12b-1 fees applicable to Class A shares. Index source: RIMES Technologies Corp. Had fees not been waived and/or expenses reimbursed in the past, returns would have been lower. Performance shown at NAV does not include the applicable front-end sales charge, which would have reduced the performance.

Class Y and R6 shares have no sales charge; therefore performance is at NAV. Class Y shares are available only to certain investors. Class R6 shares are closed to most investors. Please see the prospectus for more details.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • The first quarter of 2025 was marked by volatility and shifts in market leadership. Developed markets in Europe outperformed US equities. German equities had robust gains, driven by increased deficit spending aimed at supporting economic growth and defense. Despite ongoing political issues, French equities also posted positive double-digit returns in US dollar terms. UK equities posted positive returns as well, aided by the fact that many UK companies reported robust earnings, boosting investor confidence. Conversely, US equities declined, affected by apparent uncertainty about US trade policy and the rise of Chinese artificial intelligence company DeepSeek, which put downward pressure on US technology and consumer discretionary sectors. In this market environment, commodities, especially gold, had robust returns. Value stocks outperformed, while small-cap stocks lagged.
  • Our team remains focused on applying our long-term, bottom-up EQV investment process that seeks to identify attractively valued, high quality growth companies. We expect geopolitical and macro concerns to remain elevated, which has historically favored our traditional fundamental approach. We remain optimistic that our conservative quality growth style is well-positioned for the current environment.

Portfolio positioning

As of 03/31/25. Holdings are subject to change and are not buy/sell recommendations.

During the quarter, we initiated positions in the following stocks:

AstraZeneca is a UK-based multinational pharmaceutical and biotech company focused on discovery and development of innovative medicines in areas including cardiovascular, oncology and respiratory. The company faced temporary setbacks due to employee investigations in China and issues with its oncology drug. Despite these challenges, we believe AstraZeneca's strong product pipeline and undervalued shares present a promising investment opportunity.

Barclays is a UK-based multinational bank with retail and commercial banking in the UK, a global investment bank and a specialist US consumer bank. We have seen improving visibility for earnings and quality after a challenging period. The company has been taking action to simplify the organization, cut costs, improve accountability and enhance capital allocation. We believe Barclays is attractively valued.

BNP Paribas is a France-based multinational bank with operations in retail and investment banking, asset management and insurance. Due to its lower sensitivity to rising interest rates and French political pressures, BNP has underperformed in a strong sector. However, we see it as an interesting prospect if rates stay higher for longer and European economic activity picks up. We believe BNP is attractively valued, with an outlook for improving returns.

Legrand is a France-based global specialist in electrical and digital building infrastructures. The company has underperformed due to weak European construction markets and France’s political uncertainty. However, we believe that will change, and we expect good results from Legrand’s faster-growing segments and a cyclical recovery in core infrastructure products.

We sold the following positions:

Coloplast is a Danish health care company that we sold due to deteriorating earnings and quality trends. The stock’s valuation has continued to trade at a premium relative to its sector, so we sold it to make way for better EQV ideas.

Rentokil Initial is a British pest-control company that we exited due to questions about cost and timing for a turnaround of poor performance in its North American business.

Aalberts is a Dutch manufacturing company that has done an admirable job restructuring its internal manufacturing base and cost optimization. However, without the catalyst of this self-help going forward, we decided to sell in favor of other industrial ideas that, in our view, offer better growth profiles and valuation.

The fund’s consumer discretionary and consumer staples holdings outperformed those of the benchmark sectors and were the largest contributors to relative performance. Geographically, stock selection in Germany, Switzerland and Sweden were notable contributors to relative return.

Conversely, the largest detractors from relative return were stock selection in health care, industrials, financials and communication services. An underweight in financials hampered relative return as well. Geographically, fund holdings in France and the UK underperformed those of the benchmark index and were among the largest detractors from relative return. Exposure in

Israel, a country not represented in the benchmark, also detracted from relative results.

Contributors to performance

Below are the largest contributors to absolute return for the quarter:

Deutsche Boerse is a German stock exchange operator that benefited from increased trading volumes and strong financial results.

flatexDEGIRO is a leading pan-European discount broker. The stock performed well due to customer growth, increased assets under custody, and higher transaction volumes.

BAE Systems is a UK-based aerospace, defense and information security company that has benefited from rising global defense spending, given increasing NATO budgets. The company’s record backlog provides in our view good visibility for earnings and cash flow growth.

Detractors from performance

Below are the largest detractors from absolute return for the quarter:

Teva Pharmaceutical is a leading generics and pharmaceuticals company. The stock declined after a disappointing earnings update regarding profitability, which was affected by higher costs. Our thesis of improving earnings and quality as Teva’s management focuses on building out its innovative pharma portfolio remains intact. Valuation relative to earnings potential remains attractive, in our view.

Novo Nordisk is a Danish pharmaceuticals company. The stock fell after a couple of rare disappointments in its product development pipeline and weaker-than-expected prescription trends for its leading obesity drug, which has been under supply constraints. We trimmed the fund’s position to manage exposure.

ICON is a multinational clinical research organization. Weaker spending by large pharmaceutical firms and a challenging outlook for biotech funding have affected ICON’s short-term growth and earnings prospects, negatively affecting the stock. While demand has remained a bit challenged, the recent earnings update showed some signs of improvement in ICON’s environment, while its lower valuation appears to reflect a lot of the transitory headwinds that Icon and its industry have been facing.

Invesco EQV European Equity Fund Q1 2025 by Invesco US
